text: In this video, we attempt to describe the stepwise protocol of performing a type A radical hysterectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection for a case of cancer endometrium stage Ia that is endometrioid grade II.
We also attempt to discuss the need and the utility of the new Querlu and Moro classification and its advantages over the past various classifications of radical hysterectomy.
We will therefore take a few minutes to recapitulate this new classification and its anatomical basis so that we understand the surgery well.
This is also followed by a systematic pelvic lymphadenectomy which is also discussed taking its anatomical limits and landmarks.
So we have the Coelho and the Moro classification which was accepted in the year two thousand and eight. Through this a worldwide standard was given.

Basically the new classification came up with the realization that the Pivar-Rutledge classification did not have fixed anatomical landmarks which serve as a basis for demarcating the extent of radicality. As a result, two surgeons performing the same type III radical procedure would end up with different specimen limits. Because of a lack of clarity, there was a different interpretation in each one's concept.

Also, we saw that the old classification did not take into account the laparoscopic or the minimally invasive approach and also did not take into account the nerve sparing approach and procedures like radical trachelectomy all of which have a place in modern oncology.

Coming to the classification, we will now see that it has four main classes which are based on very well defined anatomical landmarks. We also note that the term paracervix or paracervical lymphadenectomy is added.

So what are the landmarks of this new classification? Basically we have four classes which are based on three anatomical landmarks. These are the ureter, the uterine artery, at its origin and the pelvic side wall. On the basis of these three anatomical structures radical hysterectomy has been divided into four major groups. So this picture shows us a depiction of these three landmarks.

The video displays the ureter, highlighted by a green arrow, coursing through the pararectal fossa. The uterine artery is marked with a red dot as it emerges from the anterior division of the internal iliac artery. The umbilical artery is also visible, extending superiorly.

The video displays the pelvic side wall marked by a yellow asterisk, highlighting the third anatomical landmark. The three landmarks are visually represented as levels of surgical planes: level one is the ureter, level two includes divisions of the anterior division of the internal iliac, and level three encompasses the external iliac and pelvic sidewall.
So basically if you consider any radical hysterectomy There are four things which are taken into account when describing the radicality of that surgery.

The first and the most important is the level of resection of the paracervix or which was also known as the parametra. The second is the level at which the bladder and the rectal pillars have been taken. The third is the vaginal margin that has been taken and the fourth is the What is the overall goal of the procedure?
Basically simply dividing the Paracervix resection into four types, this classification has four main groups. It is type A, B, C and D as is shown in this present picture.

The video shows a diagram illustrating type A radical hysterectomy, where the paracervix is resected at a level between the cervix and the ureter, indicated by a green line. Type B radical hysterectomy is highlighted in yellow.

The surgeon unroofs the ureter and lateralizes it at the level of the ureteric tunnel, indicated by a yellow line. In type C radical hysterectomy, the parametra is resected at the level of the uterine artery's origin from the anterior division of the internal iliac, marked by a blue line.
In type D radical hysterectomy, the parametra is resected nearly to the pelvic side wall, exposing the area up to the sciatic nerve root.

laterally from type A to type B further going laterally in type C at the division of the internal iliac and in type D reaching as far as the pelvic side wall. So these are fixed anatomical landmarks which help us define our lateral paracervical dissection.

A detailed description of all the four types of classes of the radical hysterectomy is beyond the scope of this video. Therefore, we will just describe type A here and this is what we will see in the subsequent surgery.

The surgeon performs a paraservical resection halfway between the ureter and the cervix. The bladder and rectal pillars are resected at the level of the cervix, with a vaginal margin of less than one centimeter. The goal is the complete removal of the cervix, known as extra facial hysterectomy.

The surgical team begins the hysterectomy by placing a primary 10 mm optical trocar at the umbilicus, followed by four secondary working ports. The surgeon stands on the patient's left side, utilizing two ipsilateral ports, while the assistant uses two similar ipsilateral ports on the right side.
The surgeon begins the dissection by opening the peritoneum below the right infundibulopelvic ligament, progressing towards the rectovaginal space.
The surgeon develops the rectovaginal space in the midline using a harmonic scalpel, performing precise millimeter-by-millimeter dissection close to the rectal fat.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the two layers of the Denonvilliers fascia, developing the rectovaginal space by entering the correct plane towards the fat, avoiding the vaginal part of the fascia.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the left pararectal space, using small movements of the harmonic scalpel to open the peritoneum. This reveals the ureter and traces the division of the internal iliac vascular system.

The surgeon applies traction forces parallel to the orientation of the vessels, revealing the uterine artery crossing the pararectal fossa, marked by a red arrow. Millimeter by millimeter dissection is performed, further opening the peritoneum and enlarging the surgical spaces.

The surgeon performs ligation of the uterine artery at its origin using the harmonic scalpel with a three-clip method. The deep uterine vein is visible at a deeper level, marked by a blue dot, situated below the uterine artery. The inferior hypogastric plexus, containing pelvic splanchnic nerves, is located slightly dorsomedial and deeper than the vein.
This landmark is therefore very important to identify when we are performing a nerve sparing that is type C-I radical hysterectomy.

The surgeon completes the dissection by further opening the peritoneum, focusing on developing the pararectal space on the right side. An incision is made slightly superior to the right infundibulopelvic ligament. The left hand uses a Maryland grasper to tent the peritoneum, facilitating precise dissection and allowing gas to enter and expand the natural tissue planes.

The surgeon further develops the right pararectal space, using the technique of tenting and cutting. Following this, the round ligament is opened near the pelvic side wall, serving as the gateway to the retroperitoneum during the radical hysterectomy.

The surgeon makes a cut parallel to the right infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament, skeletonizing it. The same procedure is performed on the left side, opening the round ligament laterally near the pelvic side wall and developing the left IP ligament. The surgeon then advances to the development of the vesico-vaginal space anteriorly.

The surgeon extends the peritoneum cut anteriorly, lifting the fold to develop the vesico-vaginal space and pushing the bladder caudally. The bladder pillar is taken at the level of the cervix, followed by the ligation of the infundibulopelvic ligament using a vessel sealing device.

The surgeon uses an end seal device to ligate the left infundibulopelvic ligament, while the pelvic triangle is visibly opened on the right side. The right infundibulopelvic ligament is ligated, followed by the division of the uterine artery at the level of the internal os.
The surgeon performs an extra-fascial hysterectomy, with the assistant's graspers holding the uterus steadily without any manipulation.

The surgeon demonstrates the resection level of the paracervix in a type A extrafascial hysterectomy, marked by an oblique red line midway between the cervix and ureter. The vagina is opened, and the cervix is circumferentially excised with a 3 to 5 millimeter rim of vaginal tissue, ensuring the cervix is fully included in the specimen.

The surgeon makes precise cuts into the vagina, avoiding the cervix, as part of the extra facial hysterectomy. The pelvic lymph node dissection begins with the peritoneum fold being hitched anteriorly towards the abdominal wall, providing a clear lateral stretch and freeing the assistant's grasper from maintaining tension on the fold.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the pelvic lymph nodes at the lateral border of the external iliac vascular system. Using a vessel sealing device, short bursts of energy are applied to meticulously dissect and expose the external iliac vein. The assistant uses a grasper to protect the vein on the lateral side while the surgeon delineates the boundaries to release the external iliac group of lymph nodes.

The surgeon performs pelvic lymph node dissection, defining anatomical limits: laterally by the genitofemoral nerve on the psoas muscle, medially by the ureter, inferiorly by the obturator nerve, caudally by the corona mortis (deep circumflex iliac vein), and superiorly by the bifurcation of the common iliac artery.

The external iliac vein is exposed, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the ilio obturator group of lymph nodes. The obturator nerve is identified at a deep level between the internal and external iliac vascular systems, serving as the inferior limit of the dissection. The nodal chain is removed en bloc.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection on the left side, encountering an enlarged lymph node within the external iliac group. Care is taken to preserve the nodal tissue by cutting on the fatty side, aiming to remove the node intact without disruption.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the ilio obturator group of lymph nodes, identifying the obturator nerve at a deep level marked by a yellow arrow. The deep circumflex iliac vein, marked by oblique blue lines, is visible communicating between the external iliac vein, shown by broad blue lines, and the obturator vein below. The assistant uses a grasper to apply traction on the nodal mass, stretching the lymphatics for gentle division.

The surgeon completes the closure of the vaginal vault using a number one vicryl suture on a round body needle, employing an ipsilateral suturing technique. The nodal masses are placed into an endobag and removed vaginally. The final view of the pelvis is shown after the pelvic lymphadenectomy is completed.
